topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights in the common room hummed with a weary monotony, mirroring the dull ache settling in Maya’s chest. She stared at the peeling paint on the bulletin board, the vibrant flyers for upcoming events now seeming like mocking remnants of a life that was rapidly dissolving. Every time a group of students laughed, a sharp, unwelcome tightening gripped her throat, making it hard to draw a full breath. She picked at a loose thread on her worn sweater, the fabric offering no comfort. "It's just... over," she whispered to the empty space beside her, the sound swallowed instantly by the vast, echoing silence of the suddenly hollowed-out campus. Her gaze drifted to the campus map, tracing routes she no longer cared to walk, feeling a profound weight press down on her shoulders, anchoring her to the floor.

[story 2]

Liam kept re-reading the official notices, his eyes blurring over the printed text detailing the administrative restructuring. He traced the lines of the closing announcement with a fingertip, the paper feeling brittle and inadequate against the enormity of the news. A slow, heavy sigh escaped him, a sound that seemed too loud in the quiet library. He pushed his chair back, the scrape of metal on tile echoing unnervingly in the cavernous hall. He looked around at the rows of empty desks, each one a monument to shared ambition now rendered obsolete. "So, this is it," he murmured, the words catching in his dry throat. He didn't move to pack anything; his hands remained still, clenched loosely at his sides, as if the very act of movement required too much energy.

[story 3]

Sarah walked the perimeter of the old science building for the tenth time, the late afternoon sun casting long, skeletal shadows across the cracked pavement. She stopped by a bench, sinking onto the cold metal slats. Her shoulders slumped forward, a complete surrender to gravity. She watched a few lingering students pack up their belongings, their movements efficient and final. A strange numbness spread through her limbs, making it difficult to feel anything sharp, even the sting of sadness. When a passing student offered a tentative, "Are you okay?" Sarah just shook her head once, a tiny, jerky motion, and focused intensely on a single, scuffed patch of concrete, wishing she could dissolve into the texture of the ground beneath her.
